question.question_text: Will any of Belgium, France, Italy, Luxembourg, Netherlands, and/or Germany leave the EU before 2027?
question.resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as **Yes** if, before January 1, 2027, one or more of Belgium, France, Italy, Luxembourg, Netherlands, and/or Germany triggers Article 50 of the Treaty of Lisbon by notifying the European Council of its intention to leave the union.

It also resolves as positive if by the same deadline the entity called European Union  has dissolved completely or dissolved to create a new politico-economic union of some European nations under a different name
question.background_info:
Anti-European sentiment is running strong in the EU and it may grow stronger – strong enough to destroy it, maybe? The UK voted for Brexit and political pundits are already discussing [Nexit](http://www.express.co.uk/news/world/717904/Nexit-Dutch-turn-right-euroscepticism-Geert-Wilders-Mark-Rutte), [Frexit](https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2016/jun/27/frexit-nexit-or-oexit-who-will-be-next-to-leave-the-eu) and [Quitaly](https://www.theguardian.com/business/economics-blog/2016/jul/26/italy-economy-banks-loans-crisis-europe).

Some commentators argue that Brexit is already the end of the EU as we know it, some would say that Brexit may be fine but if any other country was to leave would spell the end of the union. We choose an even stricter criterion by requiring then one of the [6 original founders of the ECSC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inner_Six) leave the union to decree the end of the EU as we know it.

### Research Summary
# Summary

The research concludes that there is no imminent exit risk from any of the six specified countries (Belgium, France, Italy, Luxembourg, Netherlands, and Germany) before 2027. All six nations remain committed EU members with no active Article 50 processes or serious dissolution movements underway. While some countries have Eurosceptic political movements—such as France's National Rally and Italy's right-wing government—these parties have either moderated their exit rhetoric or remain pragmatically engaged with EU institutions despite clashing over specific policies like budget rules and migration. The research identifies potential risk factors that could theoretically revive Eurosceptic movements, including severe economic crisis, further political fragmentation, or institutional failure within the EU itself, but assesses the current probability of any country triggering Article 50 or the EU dissolving by January 1, 2027 as very low.

## Sources

No websites or sources were cited in the provided research text.


# RESEARCH
## Report 1 Research
### Analysis: EU Exit Risk for Major Member States (2024-2027)

#### Current Situation Summary

**No imminent exit risk from any listed country.** All six nations remain committed EU members with no active Article 50 processes or serious dissolution movements.

#### Country-by-Country Assessment

**France**
- Marine Le Pen's National Rally (RN) has historically discussed "Frexit" but has moderated this stance in recent years
- Current government under PM Barnier/President Macron is pro-EU
- No credible pathway to exit before 2027

**Italy**
- Giorgia Meloni's right-wing government (since 2022) is Eurosceptic but pragmatic
- Has clashed with EU over budget rules and migration but remains engaged
- No serious exit movement; EU membership is broadly supported across political spectrum

**Netherlands**
- Consistently pro-EU; Geert Wilders' PVV won 2023 elections but formed coalition with pro-EU parties
- No exit sentiment in mainstream politics

**Germany**
- Strongly pro-EU across major parties
- AfD (far-right) is marginal in government formation
- No realistic exit scenario

**Belgium & Luxembourg**
- Both founding EU members with deep institutional integration
- No significant Eurosceptic movements threatening membership
- Luxembourg particularly dependent on EU framework

#### Key Risk Factors

1. **Economic crisis** - Severe recession could revive Eurosceptic movements
2. **Political fragmentation** - Far-right parties gaining power (though most have moderated exit rhetoric)
3. **EU institutional failure** - Complete dissolution scenario extremely unlikely but theoretically possible

#### Resolution Criteria Assessment

The question requires either:
- Triggering Article 50 (formal exit notification), OR
- Complete EU dissolution/replacement with new entity

**Current probability of either occurring by Jan 1, 2027: Very Low**

No current news suggests imminent action on either criterion.

# FORECASTS

## R1: Forecaster 1 Reasoning
### Pre-Forecast Analysis

**(a) Time left until outcome is known:**
Approximately 7 months remain until January 1, 2027.

**(b) Status quo outcome if nothing changed:**
No exit. All six countries remain EU members with no Article 50 notifications or dissolution processes initiated. This is the baseline expectation given current political trajectories.

**(c) Scenario resulting in No outcome:**
The most likely path: Political tensions continue but remain manageable within EU frameworks. Far-right parties in France, Italy, and Netherlands either moderate their positions upon governing responsibility or remain in opposition. Economic conditions remain stable enough to prevent existential crises. EU institutions adapt to pressures without fundamental breakdown. All six countries complete the forecast period as active EU members.

**(d) Scenario resulting in Yes outcome:**
A severe economic shock (banking crisis, major recession) destabilizes a large member state, triggering political upheaval. A far-right party gains unified control of France or Italy and follows through on historical exit rhetoric despite moderation trends. Alternatively, cascading institutional failures lead to EU dissolution and replacement with a new framework. Or: an unexpected political crisis (security threat, migration emergency) triggers nationalist backlash strong enough to overcome pro-EU consensus in one of these countries.

---

### Rationale

**Status quo bias is critical here.** The research assistant correctly identifies that no imminent exit risk exists. We are now in May 2026—only 7 months from resolution. This is crucial: major political changes of this magnitude typically require months of buildup, parliamentary votes, and negotiation. An Article 50 notification in the next 7 months would require either:

1. A government already in power with exit intentions (none currently exist among these six), OR
2. A dramatic political upheaval AND rapid follow-through (historically rare in EU politics)

**Key observations:**
- Marine Le Pen and the RN have *moderated* exit rhetoric, not intensified it
- Meloni's government, despite Euroscepticism, has not moved toward exit
- Wilders' coalition includes pro-EU parties as constraints
- Germany and Luxembourg remain solidly pro-EU
- Belgium has no meaningful exit movement

**The 7-month timeframe is decisive.** Even if a Eurosceptic party won elections tomorrow, triggering Article 50 within months would be politically extraordinary. Most exit movements (Brexit took years of political buildup) require sustained pressure.

**Economic risk:** A severe crisis could theoretically shift probabilities, but no such crisis is currently evident, and even crises typically take time to translate into exit movements.

**EU dissolution:** Extremely unlikely in 7 months. The EU has survived multiple existential crises; complete dissolution would require systemic collapse.

The question's strict criterion (requiring one of the six *founders* to exit, not just any member) makes this even less likely than general EU exit risk.

---

**Probability: 4%**

This reflects: baseline geopolitical stability (status quo bias), the short 7-month timeframe, moderation of far-right exit rhetoric, and the high institutional barriers to Article 50 notification. The 4% accounts for low-probability but non-negligible tail risks (severe economic shock, unexpected political upheaval, or unforeseen crisis).


## R1: Forecaster 2 Reasoning
### Pre-Forecast Analysis

**(a) Time left until outcome is known:**
Approximately 7 months remain until January 1, 2027.

**(b) Status quo outcome if nothing changed:**
No EU member state exits. All six countries remain in the EU. The union continues operating under current institutional frameworks with ongoing political tensions but no formal dissolution.

**(c) Scenario resulting in No outcome:**
The most likely path: Political tensions continue but remain manageable. Far-right parties in France, Italy, and Netherlands either moderate their positions further or fail to gain sufficient power to trigger exit processes. Economic conditions remain stable enough that exit costs appear prohibitive. EU institutions adapt incrementally to member state concerns. By January 1, 2027, no Article 50 notification has been filed and the EU remains intact.

**(d) Scenario resulting in Yes outcome:**
A severe economic crisis (banking collapse, major recession) or geopolitical shock destabilizes the EU. A far-right party gains unified control of a major member state (particularly France or Italy) and follows through on historical exit rhetoric despite moderation. Alternatively, cascading exits begin after one country triggers Article 50, or the EU undergoes institutional collapse and reorganization under a new name. This would require rapid political change in the next 7 months.

---

### Rationale

**Status quo bias is critical here.** The research assistant correctly identifies that no imminent exit risk exists currently. All six countries remain committed EU members with functioning governments that, despite Eurosceptic elements, have not seriously pursued exit.

**Key considerations:**

1. **Time constraint is tight:** Only 7 months remain. Triggering Article 50 requires formal government decision-making, which typically involves parliamentary votes, public debate, and political consensus-building. No country is currently in this process.

2. **Recent moderation of rhetoric:** Marine Le Pen and other far-right leaders have walked back explicit exit demands. The political cost of EU exit has become clearer post-Brexit, making it less attractive even to Eurosceptic parties.

3. **Institutional stickiness:** These are founding members with deep economic and political integration. Exit would be extraordinarily disruptive and costly.

4. **No triggering events on horizon:** No imminent economic crisis, geopolitical shock, or political realignment is visible that would force the issue in 7 months.

5. **Low base rate:** Despite decades of Euroscepticism, no major EU member has actually left (Brexit was exceptional and took years of political process).

**However, small non-zero risk remains:**
- Unexpected economic shock could rapidly shift political calculations
- Unforeseen geopolitical crisis could destabilize governments
- A surprise election result could bring hardline Eurosceptics to power
- The 7-month window, while short, is not zero

Given the strong status quo bias, the absence of current exit processes, the moderation of far-right rhetoric, and the short timeframe remaining, the probability is very low but not negligible.

**Probability: 4%**

question.question_text: Which party will hold the most seats in the US House of Representatives after the 2026 elections?
question.resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as the party that holds more House of Representatives seats than any other party in the 120th Congress, which is the one scheduled to be elected November 3, 2026 and take office in January 3, 2027.

Independent representatives will be counted as part of the party they caucus with.

This question will resolve as **Other** if:

* Republicans and Democrats hold an equal number of seats.
* A third party holds more seats than both Republicans and Democrats.
* Independents that do not caucus with either party hold more seats than both Republicans and Democrats.
* More seats are vacant than the ones held by both Republicans and Democrats.
question.fine_print:
Metaculus will resolve this question based on the final result of the elections, including special elections, recounts, or run-offs.

If Democrats or Republicans change name, the options will be renamed accordingly.
question.background_info:
The 2026 midterm elections are scheduled for Tuesday, November 3. The US House of Representatives has 435 seats. [As of early 2025](https://ballotpedia.org/119th_United_States_Congress), 218 are held by members of the Republican Party, 215 are held by Democrats, and 2 are vacant.

Representatives are elected for 2-year terms, and elections are held every two years; so all of the seats are up for contest in every election. The winners of the 2026 House elections will join the Senate to form the 120th Congress.

### Research Summary
# Summary

The research provides context for the 2026 US House elections by establishing the current baseline: Republicans hold 222 seats and Democrats hold 213 seats in the 119th Congress, with a majority requiring 218 seats. The analysis notes that historically, the party holding the presidency loses House seats during midterm elections, with an average loss of 20-30 seats. However, this pattern was broken in 2022 when Republicans gained seats during Biden's first midterm. Since Republicans will hold the presidency during the 2026 midterms (Trump administration), the research suggests that historical midterm dynamics could reassert themselves, though this is uncertain.

The research identifies several key factors that will influence the outcome: the Trump administration's policy impacts on voter sentiment, whether historical midterm patterns hold true, structural advantages Republicans gained from 2020 redistricting, high partisan polarization, and economic conditions and presidential approval ratings. The analysis emphasizes that no major shifts had occurred as of early 2025 to dramatically alter baseline expectations, and notes that the final resolution will depend on actual election results from November 3, 2026, including any subsequent special elections, recounts, or runoffs.

**Sources:** No external websites or sources were cited in this research document.


# RESEARCH
## Report 1 Research
### 2026 US House Elections - Current Status Brief

#### Current Composition (119th Congress, 2025-2026)
- **Republicans**: 222 seats
- **Democrats**: 213 seats
- **Total**: 435 seats (Majority threshold: 218)

#### Key Context for 2026 Forecast

**Historical Patterns:**
- The party holding the presidency typically loses House seats in midterm elections
- 2022 (Biden's first midterm): Republicans gained 9 seats, breaking the typical pattern
- Average midterm loss for sitting president's party: ~20-30 seats historically

**Current Situation:**
- Republicans currently hold the majority with a 9-seat advantage
- Democrats would need a net gain of 10+ seats to flip the majority
- The 2026 election will occur during a Republican presidency (Trump, inaugurated January 2025)

**Structural Factors:**
- Redistricting from 2020 Census favors Republicans in many districts
- Partisan polarization remains high
- Economic conditions and presidential approval will be critical variables

#### Current Indicators
As of early 2025, no major shifts have occurred that would dramatically alter baseline expectations. The question hinges on:
1. How the Trump administration's policies affect midterm dynamics
2. Whether historical midterm patterns reassert themselves
3. Specific district-level competitive races

#### Resolution Basis
This will resolve based on actual election results from November 3, 2026, including any subsequent special elections, recounts, or runoffs through the 120th Congress's formation.

**Note**: I am not providing a forecast probability, only this contextual analysis for your consideration.

# FORECASTS

## R1: Forecaster 1 Reasoning
### Analysis of 2026 US House Elections Forecast

#### (a) Time Left Until Outcome is Known
Approximately **5 months and 1 week** remain until the November 3, 2026 elections. The outcome will be definitively known by early January 2027 when the 120th Congress is seated, though results will be largely clear on election night.

#### (b) Status Quo Outcome if Nothing Changed
If current conditions persist without significant shifts, **Republicans would retain the majority**. They currently hold 222 seats versus Democrats' 213 (with 218 needed for majority). Republicans would maintain control unless Democrats achieve a net gain of 10+ seats. Historical midterm patterns suggest the sitting president's party (Republicans in 2026) typically loses seats, but the 2022 exception shows this isn't guaranteed. The status quo baseline favors Republicans holding the House.

#### (c) Scenario Resulting in Unexpected Outcome
A significant unexpected outcome could occur if: (1) major economic deterioration or a major scandal substantially damages Trump administration approval ratings in the 12-18 months before the election, triggering a larger-than-typical midterm swing against Republicans; (2) a major geopolitical crisis rallies public support around the president, defying historical midterm patterns; or (3) unexpected redistricting challenges or court decisions alter competitive district maps. The "Other" outcome (tied seats or third-party surge) remains highly unlikely given current polarization and structural factors.

---

#### Rationale

**Weighting the Status Quo:** Republicans currently hold a 9-seat advantage with structural advantages from redistricting. Historical inertia suggests they're more likely to retain control than lose it. However, the sitting president's party losing seats in midterms is the stronger historical pattern (occurring in most elections), which creates meaningful Democratic upside.

**Key Considerations:**
- **For Republicans**: Current majority, favorable district maps, polarized electorate makes large swings difficult
- **For Democrats**: Historical midterm dynamics favor the opposition party; they only need +10 net seats (achievable but not highly probable)
- **For Other**: Virtually impossible given current two-party structure and caucusing rules

**Probability Assessment:**
- Republicans have structural advantages and hold the status quo position
- Democrats have historical midterm tailwinds but face a 9-seat deficit
- The race appears genuinely competitive but Republican-favored at this 5-month mark
- Significant uncertainty remains given the time horizon and potential for major political shifts

---

#### Final Probabilities

Democrats: 0.38
Republicans: 0.60
Other: 0.02


## R1: Forecaster 2 Reasoning
### Analysis

#### (a) Time Left Until Resolution
The 2026 midterm elections are scheduled for November 3, 2026. As of May 27, 2026, approximately **5 months and 1 week** remain until the election. The outcome will be definitively known by early January 2027 when the 120th Congress is seated.

#### (b) Status Quo Outcome
If current conditions persist without significant change, **Republicans would retain the majority**. They currently hold 222 seats versus Democrats' 213 (with 218 needed for majority). This represents a 9-seat Republican advantage. The status quo baseline suggests Republicans maintain control, as:
- Redistricting favors Republicans structurally
- The sitting president's party (Republicans) would need to defy historical patterns to *gain* seats
- No major realignment has occurred in the first 5 months of 2025

#### (c) Unexpected Outcome Scenario
A Democratic flip to majority control would require a net gain of 10+ seats despite being in the opposition party during a Republican presidency. This could occur if:
- A major scandal or crisis severely damages Trump administration credibility and Republican brand
- Economic deterioration (recession, inflation spike) creates strong anti-incumbent sentiment
- Abortion rights mobilization (post-Dobbs) drives unexpectedly high Democratic turnout in suburban/swing districts
- Significant Republican retirements in competitive seats create unexpected vulnerability
- A major geopolitical crisis creates rally-around-the-flag dynamics favoring the opposition

#### Rationale

Given that we're 5 months from the election with limited new information since early 2025:

**Status quo weighting**: Republicans currently hold the majority with structural advantages (redistricting, historical midterm patterns that sometimes favor the opposition but not always). The simplest forecast is that Republicans retain their current advantage.

**Democratic path to majority**: Requires overcoming a 9-seat deficit in an environment where the sitting president's party typically loses seats. While this is plausible (historical midterm losses average 20-30 seats for the president's party), it's not the base case. However, abortion mobilization and anti-Trump sentiment remain real factors.

**Other outcomes**: A 217-217 tie or third-party surge is extremely unlikely given current US political structure and the near-zero probability of independents/third parties winning 218+ seats.

**Moderate uncertainty**: With 5 months remaining, significant events could shift dynamics. I'm leaving meaningful probability on Democrats to account for potential developments (economic crisis, major scandal, turnout surge) while maintaining that status quo (Republican majority) remains most likely.

---

Democrats: 0.38
Republicans: 0.60
Other: 0.02

question.question_text: Will the sitting U.S. president’s net worth reach ≥4x its value before they became president at any point in the following years? (2025-2026)
question.resolution_criteria: Each subquestion will resolve as **Yes** if, at any point in the corresponding two-year period, the sitting president’s net worth equals or exceeds 4 times its earliest value in the year before they take office.
question.fine_print:
* Each subquestion will use the earliest archived estimate of the president's net worth from the year before they took office. If a president served for two, non-consecutive terms, the year before they assumed office for a second time will be used.
* The primary source to resolve each subquestion will be Forbes. Net worth is calculated using Forbes’ real time net worth calculator. E.g, as reported by Forbes, Trump’s net worth was[ <u>\$2.6 billion</u>](https://web.archive.org/web/20240112150444/https://www.forbes.com/profile/donald-trump/) on January 12, 2024. Since Forbes does not provide historical data, the Internet Archive’s[ <u>Wayback Machine</u>](https://web.archive.org/) will be used to determine if/when a president crosses or equals the corresponding threshold.
* If data from Forbes are not available, we will instead use estimates from the Bloomberg Billionaires Index. If neither are available or lack an updated estimate for the relevant president, the subquestion will resolve according to required financial disclosures filed by candidates for and holders of federal office and other credible sources.
question.background_info: [<u>For Donald Trump, Forbes estimated his net worth at \$2.6 billion on January 12, 2024</u>](https://web.archive.org/web/20240112150444/https://www.forbes.com/profile/donald-trump/). [<u>The Forbes 400 estimates</u>](https://www.forbes.com/forbes-400/) of Trump’s net worth (released each fall) were \$4.3 billion in 2024 and \$7.3 billion in 2025. There is no credible record of a U.S. president’s personal wealth quadrupling during their presidency in history, but [Trump’s holdings in Trump Media & Technology Group](https://www.forbes.com/sites/danalexander/article/the-definitive-networth-of-donaldtrump/) (DJT) could provide a vehicle for unprecedented wealth accumulation.

# Summary

The research examines whether Donald Trump's net worth will reach at least 4 times its pre-presidency value during 2025-2026. According to the analysis, Trump's baseline net worth in early 2016 (before taking office in 2017) was approximately $3.5 billion according to Forbes, establishing a 4x threshold of $14 billion. However, as of early 2025, Forbes estimates his net worth at approximately $2.6 billion, which is actually lower than his pre-presidency baseline. The research notes that Trump's primary assets include real estate holdings, Trump Media stock (which is highly volatile), and golf properties.

The analysis concludes that reaching the $14 billion threshold appears highly unlikely during the 2025-2026 timeframe. This assessment is based on three key factors: his current net worth is already below 2016 levels, there are no apparent major catalysts for a $10+ billion wealth increase within two years, and Trump Media stock remains volatile and speculative. The research indicates that current trajectory suggests the resolution would be "NO," though this remains dependent on market conditions and asset valuations during the specified period.

**Sources used:**
- Forbes (real-time net worth calculator and 2016 estimates)


# RESEARCH
## Report 1 Research
### Analysis: Presidential Net Worth 4x Threshold (2025-2026)

#### Current Situation
Donald Trump is the sitting U.S. president as of January 2025.

#### Baseline Net Worth (Pre-Presidency)
According to the resolution criteria, we need Trump's earliest net worth estimate from 2016 (the year before he took office in 2017).

**Key baseline data:**
- Forbes estimated Trump's net worth at approximately **$3.5 billion** in early 2016
- This serves as the baseline for the 4x calculation
- **4x threshold = $14 billion**

#### Current Net Worth Status (as of early 2025)
- Forbes' real-time net worth calculator estimated Trump's net worth at approximately **$2.6 billion** (as of January 2024)
- More recent estimates vary but generally range between $2.6-$3.5 billion depending on source and Trump Media (DJT) stock valuation

#### Critical Assessment

**For resolution as YES, Trump's net worth would need to reach ≥$14 billion at any point during 2025-2026.**

**Current trajectory analysis:**
- Trump's net worth has **declined** from pre-presidency levels, not increased
- His primary assets include real estate holdings, Trump Media stock (highly volatile), and golf properties
- The $14 billion threshold represents a **4-5x increase** from current valuations
- This would require either: massive real estate appreciation, dramatic Trump Media stock surge, or major new wealth accumulation

#### Preliminary Assessment
Based on current information, reaching a 4x multiplier appears **highly unlikely** given:
1. Current net worth is below 2016 baseline
2. No major catalysts evident for $10+ billion wealth increase in 2 years
3. Trump Media stock remains volatile and speculative

**Current trajectory suggests resolution would be NO**, though this depends on market conditions and asset valuations during 2025-2026.

# FORECASTS

## R1: Forecaster 1 Reasoning
### Pre-Answer Analysis

**(a) Time left until outcome is known:**
The resolution period ends on December 31, 2026. As of May 27, 2026, approximately **7 months remain** for the threshold to be crossed.

**(b) Status quo outcome if nothing changed:**
If current conditions persist, the outcome would be **NO**. Trump's net worth remains in the $2.6-3.5 billion range, far below the $14 billion threshold. Without dramatic asset appreciation or new wealth sources, the 4x multiplier will not be reached.

**(c) Scenario resulting in NO outcome:**
Trump Media (DJT) stock remains volatile but does not experience sustained exponential growth. Real estate holdings appreciate modestly. No major new business ventures materialize. Net worth stays in the $3-5 billion range through end of 2026. This is the most likely path given historical precedent and current market conditions.

**(d) Scenario resulting in YES outcome:**
Trump Media stock experiences a dramatic surge (similar to meme stock phenomena), potentially driven by business expansion, profitability announcements, or speculative buying. Alternatively, a major new business deal or asset acquisition worth $10+ billion occurs. This would require unprecedented wealth accumulation in a 7-month window.

---

### Rationale

The baseline threshold is $14 billion (4x the ~$3.5 billion 2016 estimate). Current net worth estimates place Trump at roughly $2.6-3.5 billion—meaning he needs to gain $10-11 billion in the next 7 months.

**Key considerations:**

1. **Historical precedent is strongly against this**: No sitting U.S. president has ever quadrupled their net worth during their presidency. This is a major prior.

2. **Current trajectory is negative**: Trump's net worth has actually *declined* from pre-presidency levels, not increased.

3. **Time constraint is severe**: Only 7 months remain. While markets can move quickly, a $10+ billion gain requires either:
- DJT stock to reach valuations that would make it one of the largest companies by market cap (currently it's a micro-cap)
- A transformative business deal (unlikely to materialize and close in 7 months)
- Real estate appreciation of unprecedented scale (not evident)

4. **DJT stock volatility**: While Trump Media is speculative and could theoretically surge, current fundamentals don't support a 3-4x valuation increase needed to hit the threshold.

5. **Status quo bias**: Most forecasters underweight tail events. The base rate strongly favors NO.

**However, small probability remains for:**
- Speculative stock surge (meme stock dynamics)
- Unexpected major business transaction
- Aggressive asset revaluation by Forbes

Given the 7-month window, the $10+ billion gap, historical precedent, and current negative trajectory, this remains highly unlikely despite Trump's unique position and DJT's speculative nature.

**Probability: 6%**


## R1: Forecaster 2 Reasoning
### Pre-Answer Analysis

**(a) Time left until outcome is known:**
The resolution period ends on December 31, 2026. As of May 27, 2026, approximately **7 months remain** for the threshold to be crossed.

**(b) Status quo outcome if nothing changed:**
If current conditions persist, the outcome would be **NO**. Trump's net worth remains in the $2.6-3.5 billion range, far below the $14 billion threshold needed. With no major catalysts evident and limited time remaining, the status quo strongly favors a NO resolution.

The stock would need to appreciate 5-10x from current levels. Alternatively, a major real estate deal or asset sale/acquisition worth several billion dollars occurs. ### Rationale

The baseline threshold is $14 billion (4x the ~$3.5 billion 2016 estimate). Current net worth estimates place Trump at roughly $2.6-3.5 billion—**below or only marginally above his pre-presidency baseline**, not approaching 4x.

Key considerations:

1. **Historical precedent is strongly against this outcome.** No U.S. president has quadrupled their wealth during presidency. This is not merely unlikely—it's unprecedented.

4. **Current trajectory is negative.** Trump's net worth has declined from 2016 levels, not increased.

question.question_text: When will martial law be lifted in at least 3/4 of Ukraine?
question.resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as the earliest date after May 1, 2022 when at least 3/4 of Ukraine's Oblasts (top-level administrative districts as defined by the Ukrainian government; currently the 24 oblasts and Crimea) do not have martial law declared in the entire territory by the Ukrainian government.  Any regions of Ukraine partially or fully [occupied by another state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occupied_territories_of_Ukraine) will be excluded.

If this does not occur by May 29, 2027, the question will resolve as **>May 28, 2027**. If the present-day Ukrainian government ceases to exist and/or no longer controls any territory recognized as Ukrainian as of January 1, 2022, this question will resolve as **Ambiguous**
question.background_info:
[Martial law](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martial_law_in_Ukraine) was declared in Ukraine on February 24, 2022, in response to the Russian invasion.

Martial law was originally declared for 30 days. It has since been prolonged three times: by 30, 30, and 90 days respectively. Therefore, it would end on August 25, 2022 if not prolonged again or canceled.

The decision to prolong martial law is made by the security council and the president and is subject to approval by the parliament.

Martial law can be also declared in some of the regions as opposed to the whole country, like it was [in 2018](https://edition.cnn.com/2018/11/26/europe/ukraine-russia-kerch-strait-intl/index.html).

question.title: When will martial law be lifted in at least 3/4 of Ukraine?
question.description:
[Martial law](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martial_law_in_Ukraine) was declared in Ukraine on February 24, 2022, in response to the Russian invasion.

Martial law was originally declared for 30 days. It has since been prolonged three times: by 30, 30, and 90 days respectively. Therefore, it would end on August 25, 2022 if not prolonged again or canceled.

The decision to prolong martial law is made by the security council and the president and is subject to approval by the parliament.

Martial law can be also declared in some of the regions as opposed to the whole country, like it was [in 2018](https://edition.cnn.com/2018/11/26/europe/ukraine-russia-kerch-strait-intl/index.html).
question.resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as the earliest date after May 1, 2022 when at least 3/4 of Ukraine's Oblasts (top-level administrative districts as defined by the Ukrainian government; currently the 24 oblasts and Crimea) do not have martial law declared in the entire territory by the Ukrainian government.  Any regions of Ukraine partially or fully [occupied by another state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occupied_territories_of_Ukraine) will be excluded.

If this does not occur by May 29, 2027, the question will resolve as **>May 28, 2027**. If the present-day Ukrainian government ceases to exist and/or no longer controls any territory recognized as Ukrainian as of January 1, 2022, this question will resolve as **Ambiguous**

### Research Summary
# Summary

As of the research's knowledge cutoff, martial law remains in effect across Ukraine following Russia's full-scale invasion in February 2022. The research identifies several key factors that would influence when martial law could be lifted in at least 3/4 of Ukraine's territories. Factors that could support earlier lifting include a Russian withdrawal or negotiated ceasefire, with western oblasts potentially seeing martial law lifted first, though historical precedent shows martial law is typically a temporary wartime measure. Conversely, factors supporting continued martial law beyond May 28, 2027 include ongoing active combat in eastern Ukraine with no clear end in sight, Russian occupation of significant territory, and the high threshold requirement of 3/4 of oblasts (approximately 19 of 25 territories, excluding occupied regions) needing stabilization rather than just a ceasefire.

The research notes that reaching the 3/4 threshold would require either a major Russian withdrawal followed by a stabilization period, or a negotiated settlement allowing demilitarization of most territory. Given the persistence of the conflict and ongoing security requirements, the research's current assessment suggests the question would likely resolve toward the ">May 28, 2027" outcome, with martial law appearing likely to remain in most of Ukraine well into 2024-2025.

**Sources:** No external websites or links were cited in this research.


# RESEARCH
## Report 1 Research
### Analysis: Martial Law Lifting in Ukraine

#### Current Status
As of my knowledge cutoff, martial law remains in effect across Ukraine following Russia's full-scale invasion in February 2022. The Ukrainian government has maintained martial law nationwide as a security measure during active conflict.

#### Key Considerations

**Factors Supporting Earlier Resolution (Yes before 2027):**
- If Russia withdraws or a ceasefire is negotiated, martial law could be lifted progressively
- Some regions further from active combat (western oblasts) could theoretically see martial law lifted first
- Historical precedent: martial law is typically temporary wartime measure

**Factors Supporting Later Resolution (>May 28, 2027):**
- Active combat continues in eastern Ukraine with no clear end in sight
- Russia maintains occupation of significant Ukrainian territory
- The threshold is high (3/4 of oblasts = ~19 of 25 territories, excluding occupied regions)
- Even with partial Russian withdrawal, security concerns might justify continued martial law in border regions
- Lifting martial law in 3/4 of territory requires substantial stabilization, not just ceasefire

#### Resolution Mechanics
- Occupied territories are excluded from the count, which reduces the denominator
- This makes the 3/4 threshold somewhat easier mathematically, but requires peace/stability in remaining territory
- The question requires *entire* oblasts to be martial-law-free (not partial)

#### Current Trajectory
With ongoing conflict and no clear resolution path visible, martial law appears likely to remain in most of Ukraine well into 2024-2025. Reaching the 3/4 threshold would require either:
1. Major Russian withdrawal + stabilization period, OR
2. Negotiated settlement allowing demilitarization of most territory

**Most likely current assessment:** This question would currently resolve toward the **>May 28, 2027** end, given the persistence of the conflict and security requirements.

# FORECASTS

## R1: Forecaster 1 Reasoning
### Forecasting Analysis: Martial Law Lifting in Ukraine

#### (a) Time Left Until Outcome is Known
As of today (2026-05-27), there is approximately **1 year and 1 day** remaining until the resolution deadline of 2027-05-28. The question becomes resolvable if the condition is met anytime after 2022-05-29.

#### (b) Outcome if Nothing Changed
If the conflict remains frozen in its current state with no significant changes, martial law would likely persist across most of Ukraine indefinitely. The status quo suggests resolution would occur at or near the deadline: **>2027-05-28**.

#### (c) Outcome if Current Trend Continued
The current trajectory shows:
- Ongoing but relatively stabilized conflict (compared to 2022-2023)
- Martial law maintained nationwide despite some regional variations in enforcement
- No clear path to lifting martial law in 3/4 of oblasts
- Continued trend would result in resolution near the deadline: **2027-05-15 to 2027-05-28**

#### (d) Expectations of Experts and Markets
- Most security analysts expect martial law to remain in effect through 2026-2027
- A negotiated settlement or major Russian withdrawal could accelerate lifting, but appears unlikely in near term
- Western oblasts (furthest from conflict) are candidates for first martial law lifting
- Expert consensus leans toward the later end of the resolution window
- Prediction markets (if available) would likely price this near the deadline

#### (e) Low Outcome Scenario (Early Resolution)
**Unexpected breakthrough scenario:** A rapid diplomatic resolution occurs in late 2026, with Russian forces withdrawing from most territory. Ukraine stabilizes western and central oblasts quickly, allowing martial law to be lifted in ~19 oblasts by mid-2027. This would require an unexpected geopolitical shift or negotiated settlement.

#### (f) High Outcome Scenario (Late/Maximum Resolution)
**Persistent conflict scenario:** The conflict remains active or frozen with significant Russian occupation throughout 2026-2027. Security concerns prevent lifting martial law even in western regions. The 3/4 threshold is never reached, and the question resolves as **>2027-05-28** due to continued instability and occupation of eastern territories.

---

#### Final Forecast

Accounting for:
- Current conflict trajectory and stalemate
- The high threshold (3/4 of oblasts)
- Requirement for entire oblasts to be martial-law-free
- Uncertainty about diplomatic breakthroughs
- Historical precedent that martial law persists during unresolved conflicts
- The fact that we are now in May 2026 with only ~1 year remaining

I set a wide confidence interval to account for unknown unknowns (peace deals, escalations, political changes):

**Percentile 20:** 2026-10-20

**Percentile 60:** 2027-03-15

## R1: Forecaster 2 Reasoning
### Forecaster Analysis: Martial Law Lifting in Ukraine

#### (a) Time Left Until Outcome is Known
As of today (2026-05-27), there is approximately **1 year and 1 day** remaining until the resolution deadline of 2027-05-28. The question can resolve any time between 2022-05-29 and 2027-05-28, meaning we are in the final year of the resolution window.

#### (b) Outcome if Nothing Changed
If the conflict remains frozen in its current state with no significant changes, martial law would likely remain in effect across most of Ukraine indefinitely. This would result in a resolution of **>2027-05-28** (the maximum date), as the 3/4 threshold would never be reached.

#### (c) Outcome if Current Trend Continued
The current trend shows:
- Persistent but largely static front lines since late 2022
- Gradual Ukrainian stabilization in non-occupied territories
- No major Russian advances or withdrawals
- Continued security concerns in border regions

If this stalemate persists, martial law would remain widespread. However, there is potential for gradual lifting in western oblasts furthest from conflict. Current trajectory suggests resolution in **late 2026 to early 2027**, though this remains uncertain.

#### (d) Expectations of Experts and Markets
- Most security analysts expect martial law to persist through 2026 in most regions
- Some optimistic scenarios envision partial lifting in western Ukraine by late 2026
- Pessimistic scenarios see martial law maintained through 2027
- The question creator's assessment ("likely not higher than 2027-05-28") suggests moderate confidence in resolution before the deadline

#### (e) Low Outcome Scenario (Early Resolution)
**Unexpected breakthrough ceasefire and rapid Russian withdrawal (2024-2025):** A sudden diplomatic breakthrough leads to Russian withdrawal from most Ukrainian territory by mid-2025. With major combat operations concluded and security stabilized, the Ukrainian government rapidly lifts martial law in western and central oblasts. By late 2025 or early 2026, 19+ oblasts achieve martial-law-free status, resolving the question around **2025-09-01 to 2026-03-01**.

#### (f) High Outcome Scenario (Late/Maximum Resolution)
**Frozen conflict persists with security concerns:** The war remains in a grinding stalemate with no resolution. Even as combat intensity decreases, the Ukrainian government maintains martial law in border regions and areas near occupied territories due to ongoing security threats, drone attacks, and potential renewed Russian offensives. Western oblasts might see lifting, but eastern and central regions remain under martial law. The 3/4 threshold is never reached, resulting in **>2027-05-28**.

---

#### Final Answer

Accounting for:
- The current stalemate with no clear resolution path
- The high threshold (3/4 of oblasts must be martial-law-free)
- Remaining security concerns even in non-combat zones

question.question_text: What will be Donald Trump's net approval on December 31, 2026?
question.resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as Donald Trump's net approval rating on December 31, 2026, according to data from the [Silver Bulletin](https://www.natesilver.net/p/trump-approval-ratings-nate-silver-bulletin).
question.fine_print: If data for December 31, 2026 are not published by the Silver Bulletin, this question will resolve as Trump's net approval on the latest available day in December. If Silver Bulletin ceases to publish Trump's approval rating, this question will be **annulled**.
question.background_info:
US President Donald Trump's net approval rating has long hovered between -10 and -20, with him holding an unprecedentedly [stable approval rating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_polling_on_the_first_Trump_presidency) during his first term. 

During Trump's second term, his approval has moved more substantially, starting with a positive approval rating, before dropping into a negative rating, reaching the high teens as of December 2025.

### Research Summary
# Summary

This research examines what Donald Trump's net approval rating will be on December 31, 2026, noting that as of April 2024, Trump was not serving as president but was a candidate for the 2024 election. The analysis identifies that historical net approval data from Trump's presidency (2017-2021) shows his ratings typically ranged from -10 to -20 points, with an average of approximately -10 to -15 points. The research emphasizes several key uncertainties that will significantly impact any forecast, including the outcome of the 2024 election (which would determine whether Trump is measured as a sitting president or non-incumbent), the substantial time horizon of 2.75 years allowing for major political shifts, and the continuity of data sources like the Silver Bulletin tracking approval ratings through the end of 2026.

The research concludes that a definitive forecast cannot be determined without knowing the results of the 2024 election, the political events occurring between 2024 and December 2026, and which polling methodology will be used by that time. It recommends that forecasting this metric requires scenario analysis based on election outcomes and assumptions about major political events, with a wide range of plausible outcomes estimated between -25 to +10 net approval points.

**Sources used:**
- The Silver Bulletin (Nate Silver's publication)


# RESEARCH
## Report 1 Research
### Analysis for Superforecaster: Trump's Net Approval on December 31, 2026

#### Current Context

**As of my knowledge cutoff (April 2024):**
- Donald Trump is not currently serving as president
- He is a candidate for the 2024 presidential election
- Historical net approval data shows Trump's ratings have typically ranged from -10 to -20 points during his presidency
- The Silver Bulletin (Nate Silver's publication) tracks approval ratings as a key metric

#### Key Uncertainties Affecting This Forecast

1. **Electoral Outcome (2024)**: Whether Trump wins, loses, or doesn't run will dramatically affect his approval trajectory
- If elected: His approval as sitting president will be measured
- If not elected: His approval as a non-incumbent will likely differ

2. **Time Horizon**: December 31, 2026 is approximately 2.75 years away, allowing substantial time for political events to shift approval

3. **Data Source Continuity**: The question depends on Silver Bulletin continuing to publish Trump approval ratings through end of 2026

#### Historical Baseline

Trump's net approval during his presidency (2017-2021) averaged approximately -10 to -15 points, with significant variation based on events and polling methodology.

#### Current Resolution Status

**Cannot determine current resolution direction** without knowing:
- 2024 election results
- Political events between now and December 2026
- Which polling aggregation methodology Silver Bulletin uses at that time

#### Recommendation

This forecast requires scenario analysis based on 2024 election outcomes and assumptions about major political events in 2025-2026. The range of plausible outcomes is wide (-25 to +10 net approval).

# FORECASTS

## R1: Forecaster 1 Reasoning
### Analysis: Trump's Net Approval on December 31, 2026

#### (a) Time Left Until Outcome is Known
Approximately 7 months remain until December 31, 2026 (from May 27, 2026).

#### (b) The Outcome if Nothing Changed
As of the knowledge update point (late 2025), Trump's net approval had reached the "high teens" negative territory—approximately -15 to -18 points. If conditions remained static through end-2026, we would expect net approval to remain in this range, around **-16 to -17 points**.

#### (c) The Outcome if Current Trend Continued
Trump's second-term approval has shown volatility:
- Started positive (unusual for Trump)
- Declined into negative territory
- Reached high teens negative by December 2025

If this downward trend continued modestly through 2026, we might see approval drift toward **-20 to -22 points**. However, trends rarely continue linearly in presidential approval—mean reversion and event-driven shifts are common.

#### (d) Expectations of Experts and Markets
- The question creator's bounds (-35 to 0) suggest genuine uncertainty about the direction
- Historical Trump approval has been remarkably stable around -10 to -15 during his first term
- Second-term volatility suggests greater sensitivity to events
- Most forecasters would expect approval to remain negative but within historical ranges

#### (e) Low Outcome Scenario (around -35 or lower)
A major crisis or scandal in 2026 (e.g., significant legal setback, major foreign policy failure, severe economic downturn, or major scandal involving the administration) could drive approval substantially lower. Trump's approval has shown some floor around -20 historically, but unprecedented events could break this.

#### (f) High Outcome Scenario (approaching 0 or positive)
Strong economic performance, successful foreign policy achievements, or a rally-around-the-flag event could improve approval. If inflation remains controlled and GDP growth accelerates, approval could improve toward -5 to 0 range. A major geopolitical success could temporarily push into positive territory.

---

#### Forecast Rationale

**Key considerations:**
1. **Baseline stability**: Trump's approval has historically shown a floor around -10 to -15
2. **Current position**: At -16 to -18 in late 2025, relatively close to historical norms
3. **Time remaining**: 7 months is short enough that major structural shifts are less likely, but long enough for significant events
4. **Volatility**: Second-term approval has been more volatile than first-term, suggesting wider confidence intervals are appropriate
5. **Uncertainty**: Question creator's wide bounds (-35 to 0) reflect genuine uncertainty

**Distribution logic:**
- Central tendency around -15 to -18 (historical baseline + recent trend)
- Downside risk to -25/-30 range from potential crises
- Upside potential to -8/-10 from economic improvement or events
- Question creator's bounds suggest they see meaningful probability of outcomes beyond historical norms

I'm setting wide intervals to account for:
- Unknown political events in next 7 months
- Potential for approval volatility
- Uncertainty in polling methodology/aggregation
- Possibility of unprecedented scenarios

---

question.question_text: What will the average interest rate on the US federal government's total debt be at the end of these fiscal years? (FY 2026 (ending Sep 30, 2026))
question.resolution_criteria: These subquestions resolve as the average interest rate amount, expressed as a percent, paid by the United States government on its total interest-bearing debt [according to the US Treasury](https://fiscaldata.treasury.gov/interest-expense-avg-interest-rates/) at the end of each respective fiscal year.
question.background_info:
As of the end of FY 2024, the US government had \$35.46 trillion in debt. It made over \$1.1 trillion in interest payments, one of the highest spending items for the year: 

| Name                                                 | Obligated Amount        | Percent of Total |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ---------------- |
| Medicare                                             | \$1,582,650,103,296     | 16.34%           |
| Social Security                                      | \$1,541,027,236,655     | 15.91%           |
| National Defense                                     | \$1,358,253,371,219     | 14.03%           |
| **Net Interest on national debt**                    | **\$1,149,557,674,231** | **11.87%**       |
| Health                                               | \$1,098,419,579,301     | 11.34%           |
| Income Security                                      | \$717,980,743,457       | 7.41%            |
| General Government                                   | \$477,396,451,071       | 4.93%            |
| Veterans Benefits and Services                       | \$368,773,705,656       | 3.81%            |
| Education, Training, Employment, and Social Services | \$270,345,605,294       | 2.79%            |
| Transportation                                       | \$176,918,553,558       | 1.83%            |

([source](https://www.usaspending.gov/explorer/budget_function))

Notably, The average interest rate on the US national debt at year-end was 3.32%. Thirty years ago, in 1995, the average interest paid on US government securities [was](https://www.federalreserve.gov/boarddocs/rptcongress/ann95.pdf) 6.34%. Should interest on US government debt rise back to that level, the government in 2024 numbers would be making about \$2.25 trillion in interest payments, making it by far the largest spending item, larger than the current largest items of Social Security (\$1.5 trillion), Medicare (\$1.6 trillion), and national defense (\$1.4 trillion). Should interest paid rise to 10%, which were levels reached [in 1982](https://www.cbpp.org/research/federal-budget/deficits-debt-and-interest), interest on the national debt would be \$3.5 trillion, which would be 71% of the 4.92 trillion in revenue the government collected in 2024.

In the past decade, the history of the US government's debt, end-of-year interest rate, and interest obligation for the next year are as follows:

| FY   | Debt (USD trillions) | Avg Interest Rate (EoY) | Next-Year Interest Obligation (USD trillions) |
| ---- | -------------------- | ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| 2015 | 24.07                | 2.35%                   | 0.57                                          |
| 2016 | 25.59                | 2.23%                   | 0.57                                          |
| 2017 | 25.89                | 2.28%                   | 0.59                                          |
| 2018 | 26.90                | 2.49%                   | 0.67                                          |
| 2019 | 27.93                | 2.49%                   | 0.70                                          |
| 2020 | 32.67                | 1.77%                   | 0.58                                          |
| 2021 | 32.71                | 1.61%                   | 0.53                                          |
| 2022 | 32.89                | 2.07%                   | 0.68                                          |
| 2023 | 34.13                | 2.97%                   | 1.01                                          |
| 2024 | 35.46                | 3.32%                   | 1.18                                          |

### Research Summary
# Summary

The research examines factors that will influence the average interest rate on US federal debt by the end of FY 2026 (September 30, 2026), using the FY 2024 baseline of 3.324% as a starting point. The analysis identifies several key drivers: the Federal Reserve's current rate range of 5.25-5.50%, market expectations for gradual rate cuts through 2025-2026, and 10-year Treasury yields around 4.0-4.3%. The average rate is shaped by the weighted maturity mix of outstanding debt, new issuance rates as debt matures and is refinanced, and the ongoing replacement of lower-rate debt from the pre-2022 era with debt issued at higher 2022-2024 rates. The research notes a historical trend of rising rates, from approximately 2.9% in FY 2023 to 3.324% in FY 2024, and projects that by FY 2026, the refinancing effect of higher-rate debt will continue to exert upward pressure, though potential modest Fed rate cuts could provide some moderation.

Based on the continuation of an elevated rate environment, gradual refinancing dynamics, and expected modest rate cuts rather than dramatic declines, the research identifies the most likely range for FY 2026 as **3.4% - 3.7%**, representing a modest increase from the FY 2024 baseline.

**Sources:**
- US Treasury Fiscal Data (fiscaldata.treasury.gov)


# RESEARCH
## Report 1 Research
### Analysis: Average Interest Rate on US Federal Debt - FY 2026

#### Current Baseline
- **FY 2024 (9/30/24)**: 3.324% average interest rate on total interest-bearing debt
- **Source**: US Treasury Fiscal Data (fiscaldata.treasury.gov)

#### Key Factors Affecting FY 2026 Rate

##### Interest Rate Environment
- **Current Fed Policy**: The Federal Reserve has maintained rates in the 5.25-5.50% range (as of late 2024)
- **Market Expectations**: Most forecasters expect gradual rate cuts through 2025-2026, but the terminal rate remains uncertain
- **Yield Curve**: 10-year Treasury yields currently around 4.0-4.3%, which influences new debt issuance costs

##### Debt Composition Dynamics
The average rate depends on:
1. **Weighted maturity mix** of outstanding debt
2. **New issuance rates** as maturing debt is rolled over
3. **Refinancing needs** - significant portion of debt matures annually

##### Historical Context
- FY 2023: ~2.9% (lower rate environment)
- FY 2024: 3.324% (rising from refinancing at higher rates)
- **Trend**: Rising as higher-rate debt replaces lower-rate debt from pre-2022 era

##### Projection Considerations for FY 2026
- **Refinancing effect**: By FY 2026, more debt issued at 2022-2024 rates will be in the portfolio
- **If rates decline**: Could moderate the upward pressure, but won't reverse it significantly
- **If rates stay elevated**: Average rate could continue rising toward 3.5-3.8% range
- **Debt growth**: Larger total debt means more interest expense, though this doesn't directly affect the *average* rate

#### Current Assessment
Based on:
- Continuation of elevated rate environment through 2025
- Gradual refinancing of older, lower-yielding debt
- Expected modest rate cuts in 2025-2026 (not dramatic declines)

**Most likely range for FY 2026**: **3.4% - 3.7%**

This represents a modest increase from the 3.324% baseline, reflecting the lag effect of refinancing debt at rates higher than the historical average, partially offset by potential Fed rate cuts.

---

**Note**: The exact resolution will depend on Treasury's official calculation methodology and actual Fed policy outcomes through mid-2026.

# FORECASTS

## R1: Forecaster 1 Reasoning
### Analysis: Average Interest Rate on US Federal Debt - FY 2026

#### (a) Time Left Until Resolution
**~4 months** (FY 2026 ends September 30, 2026; today is May 27, 2026)

The outcome will be known when the US Treasury publishes the official average interest rate on total interest-bearing debt for the fiscal year ending 9/30/2026, likely in October 2026.

#### (b) Outcome if Nothing Changed
If interest rates and debt composition remained static at current levels:
- **Expected rate**: ~3.4-3.5%
- This represents minimal change from FY 2024's 3.324%, as the refinancing dynamics would stabilize

#### (c) Outcome if Current Trend Continued
The trend from FY 2023-2024 shows rising rates (2.97% → 3.324%) due to:
- Refinancing older, lower-yielding debt at higher current rates
- Fed maintaining elevated rates through 2025
- Continued debt accumulation at higher rates

**If this trend continues**: 3.5-3.7% range is most likely
- This reflects the lag effect of the 2022-2024 high-rate issuance entering the portfolio
- The refinancing wave from the 2010-2021 low-rate era is largely complete

#### (d) Expectations of Experts and Markets

**Congressional Budget Office (2024 projection)**:
- FY 2025: 3.4% (0.1pp increase from FY 2024)
- FY 2026-2030: Generally steady around 3.3-3.4%, with slight decline to 3.3% in 2027-2030

**Market expectations as of May 2026**:
- Fed has likely cut rates modestly from the 5.25-5.50% range maintained through 2024-2025
- 10-year Treasury yields likely in the 3.8-4.2% range
- Market consensus suggests modest rate cuts in 2025-2026, but not dramatic declines
- This would support a rate in the 3.3-3.5% range for FY 2026

**Consensus view**: 3.3-3.5%, with 3.4% as the central estimate

#### (e) Low Outcome Scenario
**Unexpected sharp Fed rate cuts or economic shock**:
- If the Fed aggressively cuts rates in response to recession/deflation in 2025-2026
- If Treasury yields fall to 2.5-3.0% range
- If debt maturity structure shifts toward longer-term issuance at lower rates
- **Result**: Could see 2.8-3.1% range
- **Probability**: ~15-20% (would require significant economic deterioration)

#### (f) High Outcome Scenario
**Persistent inflation and elevated rates**:
- If inflation remains sticky and Fed maintains rates at 4.5-5.0% through FY 2026
- If geopolitical tensions or fiscal concerns push Treasury yields higher
- If the Fed signals higher "terminal rate" than currently expected
- If debt maturity structure shifts toward shorter-term issuance at elevated rates
- **Result**: Could see 3.8-4.3% range
- **Probability**: ~15-20% (would require inflation persistence or policy shift)

---

#### Forecast Rationale

Given that we are only 4 months from resolution, the outcome is largely determined by:
1. **Current debt portfolio composition** (mostly locked in)
2. **Refinancing rates for maturing debt in May-September 2026** (partially known)
3. **Fed policy through September 2026** (mostly determined)

The CBO's projection of 3.4% for FY 2025 and steady rates thereafter aligns with market expectations. The trend from FY 2024 (3.324%) suggests a modest increase to 3.4-3.5% is most likely.

However, as a good forecaster, I acknowledge:
- Uncertainty in Fed policy outcomes
- Potential for unexpected economic shocks
- Refinancing dynamics can shift rapidly
- The wide bounds (1.0%-7.0%) suggest significant tail risk scenarios exist

I set a relatively tight distribution around the 3.3-3.6% range with modest tails to account for unknown unknowns.

---

**Percentile 10: 2.9**
**Percentile 20: 3.1**
**Percentile 40: 3.3**
**Percentile 60: 3.5**
**Percentile 80: 3.7**
**Percentile 90: 4.1**


## R1: Forecaster 2 Reasoning
### Analysis: Average Interest Rate on US Federal Debt - FY 2026

#### (a) Time Left Until Outcome is Known
**~4 months** (from May 27, 2026 to September 30, 2026)

The resolution date is essentially upon us. We are already in FY 2026 (which began October 1, 2025), and we're only ~4 months away from the official measurement date of September 30, 2026.

#### (b) Outcome if Nothing Changed
If interest rates and debt composition remained static at FY 2024 levels:
- **3.324%** (the baseline from 9/30/24)

However, this is not realistic given the dynamic nature of debt refinancing.

#### (c) Outcome if Current Trend Continued
The trend from FY 2023 to FY 2024 showed an increase of approximately 0.42 percentage points (2.90% → 3.32%).

If this trend continued at a similar pace through FY 2026:
- **FY 2025 estimate**: ~3.7-3.8%
- **FY 2026 estimate**: ~4.1-4.2%

However, this assumes no moderation in rate environment, which is unlikely given Fed rate cut expectations.

#### (d) Expectations of Experts and Markets

**Congressional Budget Office (2024 projection)**:
- FY 2025: 3.4% (0.1pp increase from FY 2024)
- FY 2026-2027: 3.3% (slight decline as rates stabilize)
- This suggests a peak around FY 2025, then modest decline

**Market consensus** (as of May 2026):
- Fed has likely cut rates from the 5.25-5.50% range into the 4.5-5.0% range
- 10-year Treasury yields have declined modestly from late 2024 levels
- Refinancing of maturing debt continues at elevated but not peak rates
- **Expected range**: 3.4% - 3.7%

**Refinancing dynamics**:
- Debt issued in 2022-2024 at higher rates is now embedded in the portfolio
- Older, lower-yielding debt (pre-2022) continues to mature and be replaced
- The weighted average continues to drift upward, but at a decelerating pace

#### (e) Low Outcome Scenario
**Scenario: Aggressive Fed Rate Cuts + Deflationary Pressures**

If the Fed aggressively cut rates in 2025-2026 due to economic weakness or recession:
- New Treasury issuance rates could fall to 3.0-3.5% range
- Rapid refinancing of maturing debt at lower rates
- Deflationary environment reduces TIPS yields
- **Potential outcome**: 2.8% - 3.1%

This would require a significant economic shock and policy reversal from current expectations.

#### (f) High Outcome Scenario
**Scenario: Persistent Inflation + Higher-for-Longer Rates**

If inflation remains sticky and the Fed maintains elevated rates longer than expected:
- Treasury yields remain in the 4.5-5.0% range through FY 2026
- Refinancing continues at elevated rates
- Debt growth accelerates, requiring more issuance at high rates
- **Potential outcome**: 3.9% - 4.3%

This would represent a continuation of the upward trend without the moderating effect of rate cuts.

---

#### Forecast Rationale

Given that we are only 4 months from resolution, much of the uncertainty has already been resolved by actual Fed policy and Treasury issuance data through May 2026. The CBO's projection of 3.3-3.4% for FY 2026 appears reasonable as a central estimate, with modest upside risk if rates remain elevated and downside risk if cuts accelerate.

The refinancing lag effect suggests the rate will be higher than FY 2024's 3.324%, but the expected Fed rate cuts should prevent a dramatic spike. A range of 3.3% to 3.8% captures most plausible outcomes, with the central tendency around 3.5%.

I'm setting a wide 90/10 interval to account for:
- Potential data surprises in the final months
- Possible revisions to Treasury's calculation methodology
- Unexpected economic shocks
- Uncertainty in the exact composition of debt maturing/being issued

---

**Percentile 20: 3.1**
**Percentile 40: 3.4**
**Percentile 60: 3.6**
**Percentile 80: 3.8**
**Percentile 90: 4.1**

group_of_questions.description:
As of the end of FY 2024, the US government had \$35.46 trillion in debt. It made over \$1.1 trillion in interest payments, one of the highest spending items for the year: 

| Name                                                 | Obligated Amount        | Percent of Total |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ---------------- |
| Medicare                                             | \$1,582,650,103,296     | 16.34%           |
| Social Security                                      | \$1,541,027,236,655     | 15.91%           |
| National Defense                                     | \$1,358,253,371,219     | 14.03%           |
| **Net Interest on national debt**                    | **\$1,149,557,674,231** | **11.87%**       |
| Health                                               | \$1,098,419,579,301     | 11.34%           |
| Income Security                                      | \$717,980,743,457       | 7.41%            |
| General Government                                   | \$477,396,451,071       | 4.93%            |
| Veterans Benefits and Services                       | \$368,773,705,656       | 3.81%            |
| Education, Training, Employment, and Social Services | \$270,345,605,294       | 2.79%            |
| Transportation                                       | \$176,918,553,558       | 1.83%            |

([source](https://www.usaspending.gov/explorer/budget_function))

Notably, The average interest rate on the US national debt at year-end was 3.32%. Thirty years ago, in 1995, the average interest paid on US government securities [was](https://www.federalreserve.gov/boarddocs/rptcongress/ann95.pdf) 6.34%. Should interest on US government debt rise back to that level, the government in 2024 numbers would be making about \$2.25 trillion in interest payments, making it by far the largest spending item, larger than the current largest items of Social Security (\$1.5 trillion), Medicare (\$1.6 trillion), and national defense (\$1.4 trillion). Should interest paid rise to 10%, which were levels reached [in 1982](https://www.cbpp.org/research/federal-budget/deficits-debt-and-interest), interest on the national debt would be \$3.5 trillion, which would be 71% of the 4.92 trillion in revenue the government collected in 2024.

In the past decade, the history of the US government's debt, end-of-year interest rate, and interest obligation for the next year are as follows:

| FY   | Debt (USD trillions) | Avg Interest Rate (EoY) | Next-Year Interest Obligation (USD trillions) |
| ---- | -------------------- | ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| 2015 | 24.07                | 2.35%                   | 0.57                                          |
| 2016 | 25.59                | 2.23%                   | 0.57                                          |
| 2017 | 25.89                | 2.28%                   | 0.59                                          |
| 2018 | 26.90                | 2.49%                   | 0.67                                          |
| 2019 | 27.93                | 2.49%                   | 0.70                                          |
| 2020 | 32.67                | 1.77%                   | 0.58                                          |
| 2021 | 32.71                | 1.61%                   | 0.53                                          |
| 2022 | 32.89                | 2.07%                   | 0.68                                          |
| 2023 | 34.13                | 2.97%                   | 1.01                                          |
| 2024 | 35.46                | 3.32%                   | 1.18                                          |

A notable forecast about the trajectory of the interest rate on the national debt is from the Congressional Budget Office [Budget and Economic Outlook: 2024 to 2034](https://www.cbo.gov/publication/59946#_idTextAnchor045), which says:

> Average interest rates on federal debt rise in CBO’s projections as debt matures and is refinanced. In 2025, the average interest rate on debt held by the public is projected to be 3.4 percent, 0.1 percentage point higher than it was in 2024 and 0.7 percentage points higher than it was in 2023. That rate generally remains steady thereafter, declining slightly to 3.3 percent from 2027 through 2030 before increasing again to 3.5 percent in the final years of the projection period. Changes in the average interest rate stem from interest rates on Treasury securities, the rate of inflation applicable to Treasury inflation-protected securities, and the maturity structure of outstanding securities. (For a discussion of the factors responsible for changes in interest rates and inflation, see [Chapter 2](https://www.cbo.gov/publication/59946#_idTextAnchor070).)
group_of_questions.resolution_criteria: These subquestions resolve as the average interest rate amount, expressed as a percent, paid by the United States government on its total interest-bearing debt [according to the US Treasury](https://fiscaldata.treasury.gov/interest-expense-avg-interest-rates/) at the end of each respective fiscal year.
